description: Rule-based and standards-based logic for Module 2 Accommodation Analysis. Execution follows a strict dependency chain: 2.1 (request validation) → 2.2 (undue hardship) → 2.3 (direct threat) → 2.4 (composite determination). Module 1 outputs are consumed but not re-evaluated. All standards-based rules use deterministic factor enumeration; identical inputs produce identical outputs.
execution_notes: Module 2 should not execute if any Module 1 output is 'no' (is_covered_employer, is_covered_individual, has_qualifying_disability, is_qualified_individual). Component 2.4 will short-circuit in these cases. However, components 2.1–2.3 may still be evaluated for completeness in multi-component API calls. The accommodation_determination component (2.4) aggregates all results and is always the terminal step.

threshold_notes.cost_burden_ratio_thresholds: The 1% and 5% cost burden ratio thresholds used in undue hardship analysis are judgment calls grounded in EEOC guidance principles but NOT explicitly stated as numerical cutoffs in 29 CFR § 1630.2(p) or the statute. They are operationalized heuristics derived from EEOC enforcement practice and should be reviewed. Specifically flagged for Scott's review: (1) the 5% 'factor for hardship' threshold — is this too high for small employers? (2) the 1% 'factor against hardship' threshold — is this too mechanistic for mid-size employers? (3) the $5,000 / sub-25-employee small-entity threshold — this figure is not in the regulation and should be validated against current EEOC guidance on small business burden.
threshold_notes.direct_threat_skew: Direct threat is a narrow exception to the accommodation obligation. The logic rules are deliberately weighted so that 'indeterminate' is the output in mixed-factor cases rather than 'yes'. A 'yes' (direct threat found) requires ALL four statutory factors to weigh clearly for the threat AND no viable mitigation AND procedurally adequate assessment. Any single procedural defect (stereotypes, no individualized assessment) is a defeater regardless of substantive factors.

rules.description: Valid request — made by employee, representative, or medical provider WITH identified functional limitation, for a qualifying accommodation purpose.
rules.condition: request_made_by IN ['employee', 'employee_representative', 'medical_provider'] AND functional_limitation_identified == true AND accommodation_is_for IN ['application_process', 'performing_job_functions', 'accessing_benefits_privileges']
rules.reasoning_template: The accommodation request was made by {request_made_by} and identified a functional limitation requiring an adjustment to {accommodation_is_for}. Under EEOC Enforcement Guidance No. 915.002 § Requesting Reasonable Accommodation, no 'magic words' are required — the individual need only make the employer aware that an adjustment is needed due to a medical condition. This request meets the threshold for validity.
rules.source_citation: 29 CFR § 1630.2(o)(1); EEOC Enforcement Guidance No. 915.002 § Requesting Reasonable Accommodation

rules.description: Valid request — employer-initiated interactive process. Employer observes performance issues and initiates accommodation inquiry.
rules.reasoning_template: The employer has initiated the interactive process, likely in response to observed performance issues or other indicators of a potential medical basis for difficulty performing job functions. Per EEOC Enforcement Guidance No. 915.002 § General Principles, employers may and should proactively inquire when there is reason to believe a condition may be affecting performance. This constitutes a valid entry point to the accommodation process.
rules.source_citation: 29 CFR § 1630.9(a); EEOC Enforcement Guidance No. 915.002 § General Principles; ADA National Network Fact Sheet § RA Process

rules.description: Invalid request — no functional limitation identified. The request must connect a medical condition to a workplace barrier.
rules.reasoning_template: The accommodation request does not identify a functional limitation or workplace barrier. Under EEOC Enforcement Guidance No. 915.002, an employee must do more than state a diagnosis — the request must connect the medical condition to a workplace need. However, the employer should engage in the interactive process to solicit this information before denying the request outright.
rules.source_citation: 29 CFR § 1630.9(a); EEOC Enforcement Guidance No. 915.002 § Requesting Reasonable Accommodation

rules.description: Undue hardship — automatic NO. Employer does not assert hardship.
factors_against_hardship_template: employer has not asserted undue hardship — defense not raised
rules.reasoning_template: The employer has not asserted undue hardship as a defense to the accommodation obligation. Under 42 U.S.C. § 12112(b)(5)(A), undue hardship is an affirmative defense that must be raised by the employer. Since the employer has not asserted it, undue_hardship_applies is no without further analysis.

rules.description: Undue hardship — automatic INDETERMINATE. Employer asserts hardship but has not documented the analysis. Burden of proof not met.
factors_against_hardship_template: employer asserts hardship but has not produced documented, individualized analysis — burden of proof not met under 29 CFR § 1630.15(d)
key_uncertainties_template: employer has asserted undue hardship but has not documented the factor-by-factor analysis required to meet the burden of proof under 29 CFR § 1630.15(d) — determination is indeterminate until documentation is produced
rules.reasoning_template: Under 29 CFR § 1630.15(d), the employer bears the burden of demonstrating that an accommodation would impose undue hardship. A generalized assertion of difficulty or expense is insufficient. The employer has asserted hardship but has not produced a documented, individualized analysis of the statutory factors under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p). The determination is indeterminate pending such documentation.

rules.description: Undue hardship — factor enumeration and cost burden calculation. Applied when employer asserts hardship AND has documented the analysis.

factors_for_hardship.factor_text: cost burden ratio exceeds 5% of {cost_burden_denominator_used} ({cost_burden_ratio}%) — high cost relative to facility resources under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(i)-(ii)
impact on facility operations is {impact_on_operations} — significant operational disruption weighs toward hardship under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(ii)
impact on other employees is {impact_on_other_employees} — significant employee disruption weighs toward hardship under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(ii)

small entity ({entity_total_employees} employees) with gross accommodation cost of ${accommodation_gross_cost} — small employer size weighs toward hardship under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(ii); NOTE: $5,000 threshold is an engine heuristic, not a statutory figure — flagged for review
ongoing recurring cost with burden ratio of {cost_burden_ratio}% — recurring nature amplifies cost burden analysis under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(i)
facility is geographically separate and administratively decentralized — entity-level resources may not be fully attributable to this facility under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(iii)

factors_against_hardship.factor_text: cost burden ratio is {cost_burden_ratio}% of {cost_burden_denominator_used} — low cost relative to facility resources weighs against hardship under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(i)-(ii); NOTE: 1% threshold is an engine heuristic — flagged for review
large entity ({entity_total_employees} employees, ${entity_annual_revenue} annual revenue) — substantial resources weigh against hardship under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(ii)
operational impact is {impact_on_operations} — minimal operations disruption weighs against hardship under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(ii)
impact on other employees is {impact_on_other_employees} — minimal employee disruption weighs against hardship under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(ii)
tax credits and outside funding offset ≥50% of gross accommodation cost — net cost substantially reduced by available offsets under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p)(2)(i)

employer has not considered alternative lower-cost accommodations before asserting hardship — failure to explore alternatives weighs against hardship defense per EEOC Enforcement Guidance No. 915.002 § Undue Hardship

output_determination_rules.undue_hardship_applies_indeterminate: all other combinations, OR cost_burden_ratio is null due to missing denominator

rules.reasoning_template: Net accommodation cost: ${accommodation_net_cost} ({cost_burden_ratio}% of {cost_burden_denominator_used}). Entity resources: {entity_total_employees} employees, ${entity_annual_revenue} annual revenue. Operational impact: {impact_on_operations}. Employee impact: {impact_on_other_employees}. Under 29 CFR § 1630.2(p), both facility and entity resources must be considered. The employer bears the burden of proof under 29 CFR § 1630.15(d). [Factor analysis follows.]

rules.description: Direct threat — automatic NO. Employer does not claim direct threat.

factors_against_threat: employer has not asserted direct threat defense

rules.reasoning_template: The employer has not asserted the direct threat defense. Direct threat is an affirmative defense under 42 U.S.C. § 12113(b) that must be raised by the employer. direct_threat_exists is no.

rules.description: Direct threat — automatic NO (procedural defeater). Assessment relied on stereotypes. Fatal to defense regardless of substantive factors.
factors_against_threat_template: assessment relied on stereotypes or generalizations about the disability rather than individualized evaluation — fatal procedural defect under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r); this defeater applies regardless of substantive factors
rules.reasoning_template: Under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r), a direct threat assessment must be based on an individualized evaluation of the individual's present ability to safely perform job functions using current medical knowledge and best available objective evidence — not stereotypes, generalizations, or assumptions about what people with a particular condition can or cannot do. The assessment here relied on stereotypes or generalizations. This is a procedural defeater that negates the direct threat defense regardless of whether substantive risk factors might otherwise exist.

rules.description: Direct threat — automatic NO (procedural defeater). No individualized assessment performed. Fatal to defense regardless of substantive factors.
rules.condition: direct_threat_claimed == true AND assessment_relied_on_stereotypes == false AND individualized_assessment_performed == false
factors_against_threat_template: no individualized assessment of this specific employee was performed — procedural requirement of 29 CFR § 1630.2(r) not met; this defeater applies regardless of substantive factors
rules.reasoning_template: Under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r), the direct threat determination must be based on an individualized assessment of the individual's present ability to safely perform the essential functions of the job. No individualized assessment was performed. This procedural deficiency defeats the direct threat defense regardless of the substantive risk factors asserted.

rules.description: Direct threat — automatic NO. Threat can be mitigated by reasonable accommodation. Accommodation is the answer; exclusion is not.
rules.condition: direct_threat_claimed == true AND assessment_relied_on_stereotypes == false AND individualized_assessment_performed == true AND threat_can_be_mitigated_by_accommodation == true
factors_against_threat_template: threat can be eliminated or reduced to an acceptable level through reasonable accommodation — accommodation is required; exclusion is not permitted under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r)
rules.reasoning_template: Under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r), direct threat requires that the significant risk of substantial harm cannot be eliminated or reduced to an acceptable level through reasonable accommodation. Here, the threat can be mitigated through accommodation ({mitigation_accommodation_description}). Because accommodation eliminates or reduces the risk to an acceptable level, the direct threat exception does not apply. The employer must provide the accommodation.

rules.description: Direct threat — factor enumeration. Applied when procedural requirements are met and threat cannot be mitigated.
rules.condition: direct_threat_claimed == true AND assessment_relied_on_stereotypes == false AND individualized_assessment_performed == true AND threat_can_be_mitigated_by_accommodation == false

factors_for_threat.factor_text: harm likelihood is {harm_likelihood} based on objective evidence — high probability of harm is a factor supporting direct threat under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r) factor 3
potential harm severity is {potential_harm_severity} — serious or catastrophic potential harm weighs toward direct threat under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r) factor 2
harm is imminent — immediate risk of harm weighs toward direct threat under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r) factor 4
risk duration is {risk_duration} — continuous or permanent risk weighs toward direct threat under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r) factor 1
assessment is supported by objective evidence and current medical knowledge — procedural requirements of 29 CFR § 1630.2(r) are satisfied

factors_against_threat.factor_text: harm likelihood is remote — speculative or remote risks do not constitute direct threat under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r); risk must be significant, not merely possible
potential harm severity is {potential_harm_severity} — minor or moderate potential harm does not meet the 'significant risk of substantial harm' standard under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r)
harm is not imminent — non-imminent risk weighs against direct threat under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r) factor 4
risk duration is temporary — time-limited risk weighs against direct threat under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r) factor 1
no objective evidence supports the threat assessment — absence of objective evidence weighs against direct threat under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r)

output_determination_rules.direct_threat_exists_indeterminate: all other combinations — direct threat is a narrow exception; mixed factors resolve as indeterminate, not yes

rules.reasoning_template: The employer has conducted an individualized assessment using current medical knowledge (procedural requirements met). The threat cannot be mitigated by accommodation. Assessment of all four statutory factors under 29 CFR § 1630.2(r): (1) Risk duration: {risk_duration}. (2) Harm severity: {potential_harm_severity}. (3) Harm likelihood: {harm_likelihood}. (4) Harm imminence: {harm_imminence}. [Factor analysis follows.]

rules.description: Composite short-circuit 1: Employer not covered.
rules.reasoning_template: The employer is not a covered entity under ADA Title I. No accommodation obligation exists. See Module 1 component 1.1 determination.

rules.description: Composite short-circuit 2: Individual not covered.
rules.reasoning_template: The individual is not in a covered status (e.g., is an independent contractor). No accommodation obligation exists. See Module 1 component 1.2 determination.

rules.description: Composite short-circuit 3a: No qualifying disability.
rules.condition: is_covered_employer == true AND is_covered_individual == true AND has_qualifying_disability == 'no'
rules.reasoning_template: The individual does not have a qualifying disability under any of the three ADA definitional prongs. No accommodation obligation exists. See Module 1 component 1.3 determination.

rules.description: Composite short-circuit 3b: Regarded-as only — no RA obligation.
rules.condition: is_covered_employer == true AND is_covered_individual == true AND has_qualifying_disability == 'yes' AND accommodation_rights_attach == false
rules.reasoning_template: The individual's disability coverage is based solely on the 'regarded as' prong of the ADA definition. Under 29 CFR § 1630.9(e), a covered entity is not required to provide a reasonable accommodation to an individual who meets the definition of disability solely under the regarded_as prong. The individual retains anti-discrimination protections but not accommodation rights.

rules.description: Composite short-circuit 4: Not a qualified individual.
rules.condition: is_covered_employer == true AND is_covered_individual == true AND has_qualifying_disability == 'yes' AND accommodation_rights_attach == true AND is_qualified_individual == 'no'
rules.reasoning_template: The individual is not a qualified individual — they cannot perform the essential functions of the position even with reasonable accommodation. Under 42 U.S.C. § 12112(b)(5)(A), the accommodation obligation applies only to 'otherwise qualified individuals.' No accommodation obligation exists.

rules.description: Composite short-circuit 5: Invalid accommodation request.
rules.condition: is_covered_employer == true AND is_covered_individual == true AND has_qualifying_disability == 'yes' AND accommodation_rights_attach == true AND is_qualified_individual == 'yes' AND accommodation_request_valid == false
rules.reasoning_template: The accommodation request is not properly formed. The employer should engage in the interactive process to allow the individual to clarify the request before treating it as a final denial. See component 2.1 determination.
rules.source_citation: 29 CFR § 1630.9(a); EEOC Enforcement Guidance No. 915.002 § Requesting Reasonable Accommodation

rules.description: Composite short-circuit 6: Undue hardship established.
rules.reasoning_template: All threshold coverage and eligibility requirements are met. However, the accommodation would impose undue hardship on the covered entity under 42 U.S.C. § 12111(10) and 29 CFR § 1630.2(p). The employer is not required to provide this specific accommodation. NOTE: The employer must still consider whether alternative, less burdensome accommodations would be effective. See recommended_actions.

rules.description: Composite short-circuit 7: Direct threat established.
rules.reasoning_template: All threshold coverage and eligibility requirements are met. However, the individual poses a direct threat — a significant risk of substantial harm — that cannot be eliminated or reduced to an acceptable level through reasonable accommodation, based on a proper individualized assessment. The employer may exclude the individual from this position.

rules.description: Composite positive determination: Accommodation required.
rules.reasoning_template: All threshold requirements are satisfied: covered employer, covered individual, qualifying disability with accommodation rights, qualified individual, valid request. No undue hardship defense established. No direct threat exists. Under 42 U.S.C. § 12112(b)(5)(A) and 29 CFR § 1630.9(a), the employer must provide the accommodation.

rules.description: Composite indeterminate: One or more upstream determinations are unresolved.
rules.condition: no_short_circuit_fired AND any upstream determination is 'indeterminate'
rules.reasoning_template: No negative short-circuit condition applies, but one or more upstream determinations are indeterminate. The accommodation determination cannot be finalized until the upstream uncertainties are resolved. See the factors_summary for the specific unresolved issues.
